# Code Organization Standard

## Summary

Use this when implementing, refactoring, reviewing, or planning non-trivial code changes.

The goal is human-readable, reviewable code. Agents should not dump new behavior into one growing file just because it is the fastest local edit.

When splitting, replacing, or refactoring code leaves old paths behind, also use [[Cleanup And Stale Artifact Discipline]].

## Why Agents Overpack Files

Agents tend to create oversized files when:

- the prompt asks for an outcome but does not define ownership boundaries
- adding to an existing file looks safer than creating imports, modules, tests, and wiring
- the agent optimizes for one-shot completion instead of small reviewable commits
- there is no file-size or responsibility trigger in the repo guidance
- the repo lacks clear feature folders, service boundaries, or component conventions
- the agent avoids refactoring because it feels like extra scope

This is a predictable behavior, not a sign that a large file is good design.

## Default Rule

Before substantial code edits, identify the ownership map:

- entry point or route
- domain/service logic
- UI component or view layer
- state, data access, or API boundary
- tests
- docs or runbook updates

New behavior should land in the smallest existing owner that already matches the responsibility. If no clear owner exists, create a focused module or component rather than expanding an unrelated file.

## File Bloat Triggers

These are review triggers, not absolute laws:

- adding a second unrelated responsibility to one file
- creating a new file over roughly `300` lines
- pushing an existing file past roughly `500` lines without an extraction note
- touching an existing file over roughly `800` lines without checking whether the change can be moved into a child module
- any file approaching `1000+` lines unless it is generated, fixture data, compiled output, or a deliberately centralized registry

When a trigger fires, the agent should either split the work or state why staying in the file is the least risky choice.

## Extraction Rules

Prefer extraction when one file contains multiple kinds of work:

- UI markup plus state machine plus API calls
- routing plus data transformation plus rendering
- persistence plus domain rules plus presentation formatting
- several feature variants behind one long conditional chain
- repeated helper logic that could be named and tested
- comments that exist only to explain a block inside a long function

Common extraction targets:

- `components/` or colocated route components for UI slices
- `hooks/` or state modules for reusable stateful behavior
- `lib/`, `services/`, or feature modules for domain logic
- `types/` or schema modules for shared contracts
- `tests/` next to the owning behavior or in the repo's existing test structure

Follow repo-local structure first. If the repo uses feature colocation, colocate. If it uses shared packages, use the shared package boundary. If there is no pattern, make the smallest obvious module and document why.

## Incremental Refactor Strategy

When a large file already exists:

- avoid mixing broad refactors with feature work by default
- extract behavior behind a stable interface before changing it deeply
- add characterization tests first when behavior is risky and untested
- split by responsibility, not arbitrary line count
- keep the app working after each step
- prefer one focused refactor commit or PR before the feature commit or PR when the feature would otherwise make the file worse

## Contribution Hygiene

Work should be easy to review and easy to land.

- one task should produce one focused change set unless the user asks for a larger batch
- do not leave unrelated dirty files mixed into the task result
- do not reformat or rename unrelated files
- include related tests with the change when feasible
- review the diff before handoff
- if the user asks to publish, commit and push a focused branch or use the relevant GitHub workflow skill/plugin
- if the user does not ask to publish, clearly report changed files and whether the repo is dirty or parked

## Review Checklist

Before finishing, ask:

- could a reviewer understand the change without reading a giant file top to bottom
- did the change add a new responsibility to an already-large file
- are imports and module boundaries clearer than before
- are tests close to the behavior they verify
- can this be reviewed as one focused pull request
- is there an explicit reason for any large file that remains large

## What Not To Do

- do not create one `app.tsx`, `index.ts`, `main.py`, or `service.ts` that owns the whole product
- do not hide complexity by moving everything into a generic `utils` file
- do not split code into tiny files with no domain meaning
- do not create abstractions only to satisfy a line-count target
- do not leave a dirty repo without a status note
